category: co-ferment or infused coffee described only in marketing prose

consistency.notes: All non-null, non-empty ground-truth labels are supported by the page text or embedded variant data. Price/currency and bag size are supported by the default variant data (250g at 24.50 USD). The infused/coferment-style label is supported by the statement that fresh lulo juice and panela syrup were added during fermentation.

realism.reasons:
This is quite plausible as an html-to-text render of a real specialty coffee product page. The structure is very believable: header nav, cart, roaster location, product title, price, option selectors, sensory notes, origin/process details, producer blurb, related products, variant block, and footer policy links all match common Shopify-style coffee pages. The voice is also mostly on target for modern specialty roasting copy: concise, lightly polished, fruit-forward, and process-aware without sounding completely generic.

The coffee data is internally coherent. Santander/Aratoca, Castillo + Colombia varieties, 1,700 masl, harvest timing, and a light filter roast all make sense together. The mention of anaerobic natural and fruit/co-fermentation-style handling is consistent with contemporary experimental Colombian lots. The tasting notes and recommendation to brew cooler to preserve acidity are also believable.

The strongest realism ding is the processing description: adding lulo juice and panela syrup during fermentation pushes this into co-fermented / infused territory, and many real roasters would label that more explicitly because it is commercially and ethically sensitive. Calling the process simply 'Anaerobic Natural' while describing additive fermentation feels a bit evasive and is a noticeable tell. Relatedly, the product title 'Lulo Splash' sounds marketable and plausible, but slightly on-the-nose in a synthetic way.

There are a couple smaller tells. A UK roaster showing dollar pricing is unusual unless this is a market-specific export storefront; not impossible, but a bit odd next to 'Leeds, United Kingdom.' The variant block is believable in form, though 'default_for_inference' is not something a customer-facing render would usually expose, so that line feels artificially annotated rather than scraped from a real page. Still, aside from those details, this reads close to a genuine specialty coffee listing.

label.coffee.sensory_text:
Tasting notes: lulo sorbet, pink lemonade, vanilla wafer

This small lot from Finca La Pradera is one of the most expressive coffees we have bought from Santander this season. Expect a cup that opens with sharp citrus and tropical fruit, then settles into creamy vanilla sweetness as it cools.
label.coffee.display_tasting_notes: lulo sorbet, pink lemonade, vanilla wafer
label.coffee.producer_text: Finca La Pradera is managed by Oscar Daza and his family in Santander, Colombia. Their program focuses on meticulous cherry selection, controlled fermentations and slow drying to produce highly distinctive microlots.

coffee.sensory_text: Bright, tropical and very limited. Expect a cup that opens with sharp citrus and tropical fruit, then settles into creamy vanilla sweetness as it cools.
